segments.title: Free French destroyers, capability, and logistics
segments.summary: The lecture argues that Free French destroyers offered valuable Allied capability, but availability, refits, crew access, spare parts, and sustainment shaped what they could actually contribute.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing argument. Dr. Clarke opens by framing French large destroyers as a useful Allied capability and then follows ships such as Leopard, Tigre, Le Triomphant, Mistral, and La Combattante through the uneven realities of Operation Catapult, Vichy control, Allied transfer, refits, crew shortages, and maintenance limits. The closing question turns that operational record into a broader counterfactual about whether a larger Free French naval force would have boosted Allied capability or created extra sustainment problems.

evidence.note: The introduction frames the Free French as lacking access to the whole French Navy while Allied planners still valued French large destroyers as a useful capability.
The design-context section explains the French task-group concept and the role of contre-torpilleurs as large, fast destroyers built to provide scout and combat power.
The Leopard section traces Operation Catapult transfer, Free French service, British refits, convoy escort, anti-submarine work, and the liberation of La Reunion.
The Le Triomphant section shows a valuable large destroyer constrained by machinery problems, long refits, spare-part shortages, and classification disputes.
The Branlebas section turns a ship transfer into a lesson about Operation Catapult, Allied worst-case planning, and the need to move spare parts with evacuated ships.
The La Combattante and conclusion sections use a well-maintained Free French destroyer to show what the force could do, then ask whether a larger force would have helped or strained logistics.
